steps.reveal: Improbe = O cruel/shameless (voc. masc. sg., addressing Amor)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'improbus' — the -e ending marks the vocative of 2nd decl. -us adjectives
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'improbo' — 'improbe' with -e is vocative
hints.adverb: The adverb 'improbe' does exist, but here it modifies 'Amor' as a vocative adjective

steps.reveal: Amor = O Love (voc. sg., personified Love being addressed)

hints.nominative: While 'Amor' looks nominative, the context is direct address — 'O cruel Love'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Amorem' — 'Amor' is vocative (same form as nominative in 3rd decl.)
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Amoris' — 'Amor' is vocative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'amoris' ends in -is (3rd declension)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'amoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'amoris' ends in -is

hints.nominative: While 'mortalia' could be nom. pl. neut., here it is accusative, modifying 'pectora' (object of 'cogis')
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'mortalibus' — 'mortalia' is accusative plural neuter
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'mortalium' — 'mortalia' is accusative

hints.nominative: While 'pectora' could be nom. pl. neut., here it is the accusative object of 'cogis'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'pectoribus' — 'pectora' is accusative plural
hints.vocative: Vocative would look like nominative — but 'pectora' is accusative, object of 'cogis'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'pectoris' ends in -is (3rd declension)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'pectoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'pectoris' ends in -is

hints.to think: That would be 'cogitare' — 'cogis' comes from 'cogere' (to compel/drive together)
hints.to know: That would be 'cognoscere' — 'cogis' comes from 'cogere' (to compel)
hints.to gather: That would be 'colligere' — while 'cogere' can mean 'gather', here it means 'compel'

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'cogas' — 'cogis' with -is is present indicative (3rd conj.)
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'coegisti' — 'cogis' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'coges' — 'cogis' with -is is present indicative

hints.cogito, cogitare, cogitavi, cogitatum: These are the parts of 'cogitare' (to think)
hints.cognosco, cognoscere, cognovi, cognitum: These are the parts of 'cognoscere' (to learn/know)
hints.colligo, colligere, collegi, collectum: These are the parts of 'colligere' (to gather/collect)

translation: Cruel Love, to what do you not compel mortal hearts?
